definition: Amazonian fruit served as smoothie bowls. Tupi 'ïwasa'i'. Dark purple, creamy, slightly earthy. Superfood trend.
Fried black-eyed pea fritters stuffed with spicy shrimp. Yoruba origin. Golden, crispy, savory-spicy.
Sweet, aged vinegar from Modena or Reggio Emilia. Dark, syrupy, complex. Made from grape must, aged in barrels.
Annatto seeds used for color and flavor in Yucatán dishes. Nahuatl 'achiotl'. Orange-red color, mild earthy flavor.
Adding acid to preserve color or adjust flavor. Latin 'acidus', sour. Prevents browning.
A marinade or stew made with vinegar, chiles, garlic, and spices. Spanish 'adobar', to marinate. Complex, deep flavor.
Incorporating air to lighten batters, creams, or dough. Latin 'aer', air. Creates light texture.
Seaweed-derived gelling agent used as a vegan gelatin substitute. Malay for 'jelly'. Sets at room temperature.
Developing flavor and tenderness through controlled storage. Applies to meats, cheeses, wines. Time-based transformation.
Refreshing beverage made from fruit, sugar, and water. Spanish 'fresh water'. Light, sweet, hydrating.
Garlic emulsified sauce similar to mayonnaise. Provençal 'garlic + oil'. Pungent, creamy, versatile.
A Balkan roasted red pepper spread. Made from roasted red peppers, eggplant, and spices. Smoky, sweet, slightly spicy. Condiment and sandwich spread.
Pasta cooked firm and slightly resistant to bite. Italian 'to the tooth'. Perfect pasta texture. Not soft, not hard.
A measured sub-portion used in food science. Latin 'aliquot', several. Essential for scaling recipes.
Appetizer course featuring cured meats, olives, cheeses, and vegetables. Italian 'before the meal'. First course tradition.
Fried rice balls stuffed with cheese or ragù. Italian 'little oranges'. Crispy, golden, savory filling.
Serving meat with natural cooking juices, lightly thickened or concentrated, rather than a separate gravy or sauce. Essential for roasted and braised meats in classical service.
Resting flour and water to improve gluten development. Greek 'auto (self) + lysis (breakdown)'. Enhances dough structure.
Keeping emulsions or custards from breaking using gentle heat. Culinary science. Preserves texture.
Water bath used to gently cook delicate foods. French 'Mary's bath'. Provides even heat distribution.
Slow-cooked meat traditionally prepared in a pit or wrapped in leaves. Taíno origin 'barabicu'. Tender, flavorful, complex.
Coconut truffles made with condensed milk. Portuguese 'little kiss'. Sweet, creamy, covered in coconut.
A sauce made from butter emulsified with vinegar or wine and shallots. Delicate, requires gentle heat. One of the mother sauces' derivatives.
A paste of flour and softened butter used for last-minute thickening. French 'kneaded butter'. No roux taste.
Browned butter used for sauces and pastries. French 'hazelnut butter'. Nutty aroma, complex flavor.
Spiced meat stew often served with consommé for dipping. Mexican origin meaning 'savory stew'. Rich, complex, satisfying.
French technique of briefly cooking vegetables in boiling water and immediately refreshing in ice water to stop cooking. Preserves color and texture.
Cooking food briefly in boiling water then shocking in ice to stop cooking. French 'blanchir', to whiten. Preserves color and texture.
Quick method of making puff pastry with rough-layering. Modern technique. Less time than traditional lamination.
Heating spices in oil to intensify flavor. Culinary science. Activates volatile compounds.
Shrimp stew thickened with cassava puree and coconut milk. Afro-Brazilian tradition. Creamy, aromatic, seafood-rich.
Cooking method using rapid heating where water bubbles vigorously at 212°F (100°C) at sea level. Used for pasta, potatoes, eggs, and making stocks.
A type of Chinese cabbage used in soups, stir-fries, and braises. Cantonese 'white vegetable'. Tender greens with crisp stalks. Quick-cooking.
Thin layered cake rolled with guava paste. Brazilian specialty. Delicate layers, sweet guava filling.
A meat-based pasta sauce cooked with aromatics and tomatoes. Named after Bologna. Rich, complex, slow-cooked.
Cooking method combining dry heat (browning) followed by moist heat (simmering in liquid) in a covered pot. Creates tender meat and deep flavors.
Slow cooking in a covered pot with small amounts of liquid. French 'braiser'. Creates tender, flavorful results.
Coating food with breadcrumbs for frying or baking. Three-step process: flour, egg wash, breadcrumbs. Creates crispy exterior, tender interior.
Chocolate truffle made with condensed milk. Named after Brigadier Eduardo Gomes. Sweet, creamy, iconic.
Light brown roux made with equal parts butter and flour cooked until light brown color develops but not as dark as espagnole. Used as sauce base.
Fine dice cut typically 1–2 mm cubes. French culinary term. Finest classical dice cut.
Grilled bread topped with tomatoes, garlic, and olive oil. Italian 'bruscare', meaning to roast. Crunchy, fresh, simple.
The hanger steak prized for flavor. Butchers kept this cut for themselves. Flavorful, tender.
Dish braised with tomatoes, herbs, and peppers. Italian 'hunter style'. Often chicken or game.
Distilled liquor made from fermented sugarcane juice. Portuguese sugarcane spirit. High alcohol, fruity, sharp.
Brazil's national cocktail made with cachaça, lime, and sugar. Portuguese 'little countryside drink'. Refreshing, potent.
Sweet and sour eggplant dish with capers and vinegar. Sicilian origins. Complex flavor, preserved condiment.
Sugar browning reaction producing complex sweet flavors. Occurs above 320°F (160°C). Creates deep, complex flavors.
Process of heating sugars (natural or added) until they brown and develop rich, complex flavors. Temperature-dependent: light (320°F/160°C) to dark (380°F/193°C). Essential for sauce development.
A pasta sauce made of egg, cheese, and cured pork. Italian 'charcoal worker's style'. Rich, creamy, simple ingredients.
Slow-cooked pork crisped in its own fat. Spanish 'little meats'. Tender, rich, succulent. Often served in tacos.
Marinating seafood in citrus to denature proteins. Peruvian origin. Creates cooked-like texture without heat.
Citrus-marinated seafood with tomato, cilantro, and jalapeño. Latin American coastal tradition. Fresh, bright, tangy.
Taco-like dish with a fried masa shell. Spanish 'small boat'. Crispy, topped with beans and meat.
Cantonese-style barbecued pork glazed with honey, soy sauce, and spices. 'Fork roasted'. Deep red color from marinade. Tender and sweet-savory.
Blackening food to develop smoky, bitter, and sweet flavors. Maillard and caramelization reactions. Deep flavor development.
Fine ribbon cut of leafy vegetables and herbs, typically 1/16-inch (1.5mm) wide. Used for garnish and delicate presentations.
A black rice vinegar used for dipping and braising. Named after Zhenjiang city. Complex, slightly sweet, rich umami flavor.
Smoked and dried jalapeño used in sauces. Nahuatl 'chilpoctli', meaning smoked chile. Deep, complex, spicy heat.
Spicy pork sausage seasoned with smoked paprika or chiles. Spanish origin. Can be fresh, dried, or smoked.
Stir-fried noodles with vegetables and/or protein. Cantonese 'fried noodles'. Popular globally but regional variations significant.
Brazilian grilling tradition featuring skewered meats. Portuguese for 'barbecue'. Social, meat-focused dining tradition.
Removing impurities to make a stock or butter transparent. French technique. Creates crystal-clear consommé.
Smoking food at low temperatures for flavor without cooking. Traditional Nordic method. Preserves raw texture.
Slow-cooked and preserved in its own fat. Originated as French preservation technique. Creates tender, flavorful results. Examples: duck confit, garlic confit.
Thick fruit preserve similar to jam. French 'to confect'. Rich, concentrated fruit flavor.
Slow-cooked rice porridge often served with savory toppings. Chinese 'zhou'. Tamil 'kanji'. Comfort food across Asia. Varies by region.
A clarified, highly refined broth made from stock and a meat/vegetable mixture. Clear, delicate, aromatic. Requires careful clarification process.
Heat transfer method using air movement in ovens. Latin 'convehere', to transport. Distributes heat evenly.
A coffee drink equal parts espresso and warm milk. Spanish 'cut'. Balanced, creamy.
A smooth, strained sauce made from fruit or vegetables. French 'strained liquid'. Refined, elegant sauce.
Chicken croquettes shaped like a teardrop. Portuguese 'little thigh'. Crispy, creamy filling.
Raw fish or meat served with citrus, oils, and seasonings. Italian 'raw'. Modern technique showcasing ingredient quality.
Internal texture pattern in breads and cakes. Determined by yeast, fermentation, mixing. Indicates quality and technique.
Freezing herbs before grinding to preserve color and flavor. Modern food science. Maintains vibrant color.
Spiced sauce or dish with complex flavor from multiple spices, herbs, and aromatics. Fundamental to Indian, Thai, and Southeast Asian cuisines. Range from mild to very hot.
A broth made from kombu (seaweed) and bonito flakes forming the base of Japanese cuisine. Japanese 'extract'. Fundamental umami-rich stock.
Presenting components of a classic dish in separated forms. Modernist cuisine. Creates conceptual presentation.
Adding liquid (wine, stock, water) to hot pan after cooking proteins to dissolve caramelized drippings (fond). Creates flavorful pan sauce. Requires deglazing liquid to loosen fond with scraper.
Adding liquid to hot pan after cooking to dissolve caramelized drippings (fond). Creates flavorful sauce base. Using scraper to loosen fond is essential.
Removing moisture from ingredients for preservation or texture change. Modern technique. Creates concentrated flavors and crispy textures.
Rich, glossy sauce made by reducing equal parts brown sauce and brown stock to concentrated consistency. Fundamental mother sauce derivative. French for "half-glaze".
Palm oil used in Afro-Brazilian cooking. Angolan Portuguese origin. Golden-red, distinctive flavor, culinary staple.
Small bite-sized dishes served in bamboo steamers or on small plates. Cantonese 'touch the heart'. Traditionally served with tea. Various fillings and preparations.
A rice bowl topped with meat, vegetables, or fish. Japanese 'bowl'. Quick meal, satisfying. Various regional styles and fillings.
A spicy fermented broad bean paste essential for Sichuan cuisine. Reddish-brown color, complex umami, spicy heat. Used in marinades and sauces.
Soft tofu dessert or savory soup topping. Chinese 'bean flower'. Silken texture, delicate. Sweet or savory preparation.
Coating food lightly in flour or breadcrumbs before cooking. Old English 'dragian'. Creates texture and color.
Exposing meat to controlled humidity and airflow for flavor development. Butchery practice. Creates concentrated beef flavor.
Seasoning food with salt to draw out and reabsorb moisture. Modern culinary science. Creates juicy, flavorful results.
Finely chopped mushrooms cooked with shallots and herbs. Used as filling, garnish, or sauce component. Named after 17th-century Marquis d'Uxelles.
Mexican street corn topped with mayo, cheese, and chili. Nahuatl 'elotli', meaning corn cob. Creamy, savory, spicy.
Large savory pie filled with chicken or shrimp. Portuguese pie. Crispy crust, creamy filling.
Food science process of combining two immiscible liquids (oil and water) by breaking one into tiny droplets suspended in the other. Requires emulsifying agent.
A stable mixture of two immiscible liquids, like oil and water. Science-based technique. Examples: mayonnaise, beurre blanc, hollandaise.
Baking food inside parchment to steam in its own juices. French 'in parchment'. Preserves moisture and flavor.
Using natural enzymes to soften meats. Modern food chemistry. Breaks down protein fibers.
Concentrated coffee made by forcing hot water through finely ground beans. Italian 'pressed out'. Rich, bold, complex.
Toasted cassava flour used as a side dish or topping. Portuguese, possibly African origins. Dry, nutty, versatile.
Dish of beans with bacon, eggs, and cassava flour. Tropeiro cowboys of Brazil. Hearty, rustic.
A black bean stew with pork, considered Brazil's national dish. Portuguese 'feijão', meaning beans. Rich, complex, festive.
Transformation of ingredients by microorganisms for flavor development. Creates probiotics, complex flavors. Ancient technique.
Blend of star anise, fennel, cloves, cinnamon, and Sichuan peppercorn. Represents five flavors of Chinese cooking. Sweet, savory, warm spice blend.
Igniting alcohol to create dramatic flavor and presentation. French 'flamed'. Burns off alcohol, concentrates flavor.
Rapid reduction over extreme heat for concentrated flavor. Culinary technique. Quick sauce making.
Rapidly freezing food to preserve cell structure and freshness. Modern technology. Maintains quality better than slow freezing.
Dishes prepared with spinach and béchamel. Named after Florence, Italy. Creamy, green, vegetable-forward.
Decorative crimping technique on pastries. French origin. Creates visual texture.
Soft flatbread topped with herbs and olive oil. Italian from Latin 'focus' (hearth). Airy crumb, crispy crust.
Gentle combining of ingredients (usually incorporating light ingredient into heavy). Uses spatula in cutting and turning motions to preserve air and structure.
Caramelized protein bits stuck to pan surface. French 'base' or 'foundation'. Contains concentrated flavor. Used for pan sauces.
Potatoes browned then braised in stock and butter. French 'melting'. Crispy outside, creamy inside.
Melted cheese or chocolate served communally. French 'to melt'. Interactive, social dining tradition.
A dish of meat sautéed before being stewed in a white sauce. Typically uses poultry. Tender, creamy result from gentle cooking in sauce.
Reducing liquid to syrup consistency. Culinary technique. Creates thick, concentrated glaze.
A rice seasoning blend made from seaweed, sesame, fish, and spices. Japanese 'sprinkle over'. Versatile topping.
Caramelized sugar deglazed with vinegar, used for sweet–sour sauces. French culinary term. Complex balance of sweet and acidic.
Clarified butter with all water and milk solids removed. Used extensively in Indian cuisine. High smoke point makes it ideal for high-heat cooking.
Coating or glossy finish on food, typically achieved by coating with gelatin, aspic, or glaze. Creates shiny, professional appearance.
Soft dumplings made from potatoes or semolina. Italian 'nocchio', meaning knot. Light, pillowy texture. Served with sauce.
A fermented Korean chili paste made from red chili peppers, soybeans, salt, and other ingredients. Deep umami flavor with spicy heat. Essential Korean ingredient.
Stuffed masa pocket filled with meats and toppings. Spanish 'little fat one'. Soft, comforting.
A distilled spirit made from leftover grape skins and seeds. Italian pomace brandy. High alcohol, sharp, complex.
Condiment of lemon zest, parsley, and garlic used as a bright garnish. Italian origin. Fresh, pungent, aromatic.
Pan-fried dumplings with meat and vegetables. Adapted from Chinese 'jiaozi'. Crispy bottoms, tender tops. Popular appetizer.
A North African chili paste made from roasted red peppers, chili peppers, spices, and garlic. Spicy, smoky, aromatic. Used throughout North African cuisine.
Rice-based sweet beverage flavored with cinnamon. Latin 'hordeum' (barley). Creamy, sweet, refreshing.
A simmering pot of broth used to cook raw meats and vegetables at the table. Interactive communal cooking. Popular in China, Japan, Korea.
Cooking food through exposure to smoke and moderate heat. Traditional preservation. Creates cooked, smoked result.
Gelling, thickening, or stabilizing agent used in modernist cuisine. Greek 'hydro (water) + kolla (glue)'. Examples: agar, xanthan gum.
Extracting flavor by steeping ingredients in liquid (oil, vinegar, alcohol, liquid). Can be hot or cold. Creates flavored cooking mediums.
A popular breakfast crepe filled with eggs, sauces, and crunchy wonton. Chinese street food. Thin, delicate wrapper with savory filling.
Thin matchstick-style vegetable cuts. French cut named after a chef. Uniform, fine, decorative.
Dried, smoked fish flakes (bonito) used in dashi and toppings. Japanese 'dried bonito'. Adds umami and bonito fragrance.
A fermented Korean vegetable dish seasoned with chili, garlic, and other spices. Tangy, spicy, aromatic. Fundamental to Korean cuisine. Requires fermentation.
Dried seaweed used in dashi. Japanese kelp. Essential umami source. Used in stocks and soups.
A thick, strained yogurt cheese from Middle East and Mediterranean. Creamy, tangy, versatile. Made by straining yogurt to remove whey. Can be served as spread or dip.
Baking technique creating layers of butter and dough through repeated folding. Creates flaky pastries like croissants and Danish. Requires precise temperature control.
Creating layers of dough and butter for pastries like croissants. French layering technique. Produces flaky texture.
A famous chili crisp condiment known worldwide. 'Old Godmother' in Chinese. Complex, balanced heat with savory notes. Versatile condiment.
Large pork meatballs braised in broth with greens. Shanghai dish. Named for its large size resembling a lion's head. Tender, savory braise.
Soft wheat noodles tossed with sauce and stir-fried ingredients. Cantonese 'tossed noodles'. More sauce-focused than chow mein.
A signature Sichuan flavor profile combining 'ma' (numbing sensation from Sichuan peppercorn) with 'la' (spicy heat from chilis). Balanced and complex.
Soaking foods in liquid to extract or infuse flavors. Latin 'macerare', to soften. Creates flavored liquids and softened ingredients.
Browning from amino acids and reducing sugars producing savory flavors. Named after Louis Maillard. Creates complex, savory notes.
Cassava root used in countless Brazilian dishes. Tupi origin. Versatile, starchy vegetable.
A spicy Sichuan tofu dish with ground pork and chili bean paste. Named after a pockmarked woman who invented the dish. Balance of numbing, spicy, and savory.
Soaking food in flavorful liquid (acid, oil, spices) to tenderize and flavor. Time varies: 30 minutes to overnight depending on ingredient and desired intensity.
Finely powdered green tea used in ceremonies and desserts. Vibrant green color. Complex, slightly bitter, grassy flavor. Ceremonial grade best.
Traditional soup made from beef tripe and hominy. Spanish for trifles. Hearty, flavorful, traditional.
Finely cutting food into very small pieces. Critical for pâtés, tartares, and meat preparations. Can be done with knife, food processor, or grinder.

Basic aromatic vegetable mixture: 50% onion, 25% celery, 25% carrot (ratio by weight). Foundation of French cooking. White mirepoix uses celery root instead of carrot. Essential for stocks, braises, soups.
A sweet rice wine used for glazing and seasoning. Light honey color. Sweet, slightly complex flavor. Used in sauces and glazes.
The complete setup of ingredients and tools before cooking. French for 'put in place'. Essential practice in professional kitchens.
A fermented paste made from soybeans, rice, or barley. Rich umami flavor. Varies by region and fermentation length. Key seasoning in Japanese cuisine.
A chewy rice cake used in desserts and savory dishes. Japanese 'sticky rice cake'. Glutinous texture, mild flavor. Versatile ingredient.
A citrus-garlic sauce used in Caribbean cuisine, especially Cuban. Made with lime juice, garlic, and spices. Bright, acidic, aromatic flavor.
Complex sauce made from chiles, nuts, spices, and sometimes chocolate. Nahuatl 'molli', meaning sauce. Rich, layered flavors.
A dense pastry filled with lotus paste or red bean, often with egg yolks. Traditional Mid-Autumn Festival food. Sweet and rich.
Seafood stew made with coconut milk, palm oil, and peppers. Indigenous Tupi 'moquém', meaning slow-cooked. Aromatic, tropical.
Whisking cold butter into sauce at end of cooking. Adds richness, glossy finish, and emulsified texture. French: 'beurre monté'.
The proper viscosity of a sauce that coats the back of a spoon. French 'to coat'. Essential doneness indicator for sauces.
A spreadable spicy Italian salami from Calabria. Rich, fatty, intensely flavorful. Can be spread on bread or cooked into dishes. Contains chili peppers.
Pressed sushi made by hand with rice and fish. Japanese 'to grasp'. Simple, elegant. Shows fish quality and chef skill.
The alkaline process that transforms corn for tortillas and tamales. Nahuatl origin. Makes corn nutritionally complete.
Rice balls wrapped in seaweed, often filled with savory items. Japanese 'to shape'. Portable, versatile. Classic Japanese food.
Braised veal shanks with marrow-rich bones. Italian 'bone with a hole'. Tender, flavorful, served with gremolata.
Spanish rice dish cooked in wide shallow pan over direct heat. Rice absorbs flavorful stock without stirring. Creates crispy bottom layer (socarrat).
Reducing pan liquids to make sauce. Culinary technique. Quick pan sauce creation.
A paste of bread and liquid used to keep ground meat moist. French 'bread mash'. Essential for forcemeats and mousse.
Salad made from stale bread, tomatoes, and onions. Tuscan bread salad. Uses leftover bread creatively.
Chewy cheese-filled cassava rolls. Portuguese 'cheese bread'. Airy, tangy, perfect snack.
Hard, aged cheese known as the King of Cheeses. Named after Parma and Reggio Emilia. Complex, crystalline, nutty flavor.
Fried pastry filled with meat, cheese, or hearts of palm. Portuguese pastry. Crispy, golden, versatile filling.
Light pastry dough used for éclairs and cream puffs. French 'cabbage paste'. Contains eggs and butter. Creates hollow structures.
A family of firm, salty sheep's milk cheeses. Italian 'pecora' (sheep). Various types by region and aging.
A sauce of basil, garlic, pine nuts, cheese, and olive oil. Italian 'pestare', meaning to crush. Green, aromatic, emulsified.
Yucatán dish where meat is marinated and cooked in wrapped leaves. Mayan 'pib', meaning buried. Tender, complex, slow-cooked.
Popular Brazilian cut of beef grilled over open flame. Portuguese term for top sirloin cap. Tender, flavorful, succulent.
Design principles of visual dish presentation. Modern gastronomic term. Creates visual appeal.
To gently cook food in simmering liquid. Common for eggs, fish, and delicate proteins. Temperature around 160-180°F (71-82°C).
Mild dark green chile used in many dishes. Named after Puebla, Mexico. Earthy, slightly smoky, perfect for stuffing.
A Hawaiian dish of seasoned raw fish cubed and marinated in soy sauce and sesame oil. Fresh, savory, rich. Now popular globally.
Cornmeal porridge served creamy or grilled. Italian from Latin 'pollen' (fine flour). Staple in Northern Italy.
A citrus-soy dressing used with meats, fish, and vegetables. Portuguese 'pon' (punch) + Japanese 'zu' (vinegar). Bright, acidic, complex.
Hearty soup made with hominy and meat. Nahuatl 'pozolli', meaning foamy. Festive, warming, complex broth.
Allowing dough to rise through yeast fermentation. Baking term. Creates air and flavor.
Air-dried cured ham served sliced thin. Italian 'prosciugare', meaning to dry. Salty, complex, melting texture.
Cutting fruits or vegetables into quarters. French 'quarter'. Simple vegetable cut.
Soft, crumbly cheese used in tacos and salads. Spanish 'fresh cheese'. Mild, slightly tangy, unaged.
Bright yellow coconut custard dessert. Portuguese; African influence. Sweet, creamy, coconut-forward.
Slow-cooked Italian meat sauce. From French 'ragout', to stimulate appetite. Rich, complex, aromatic.
Japanese noodle soup with meat or fish-based broths. Chinese origin but naturalized in Japan. Complex broths, various regional styles.
Unrefined cane sugar used in desserts. Portuguese term. Brown, crystalline, molasses flavor.
A braising method using soy sauce, sugar, and aromatics. Named for the reddish color from soy and caramelized sugar. Slow cooking method.
Thickening liquid by evaporation to intensify flavor. Key sauce-making technique. Concentrates flavors and creates body.
Melting fat from meat through gentle heating. Old French 'rendre', to give back. Extracts liquid fat.
Slow-cooked, shredded meat preserved in fat. French origin. Spreadable, rich, complex.
Creamy rice dish cooked slowly with broth, stirring frequently. Italian rice technique. Arborio rice most common. Luxurious texture.
Cooking method using dry heat in an oven at high temperatures (typically 350-450°F / 175-230°C) to cook foods surrounded by hot air, creating browning and caramelization.
Dessert pairing of guava paste and cheese. Named after Shakespeare's lovers. Sweet-salty, traditional.
A cooked mixture of equal parts fat and flour used as a thickener in sauces. Colors range from white (light roux) to dark brown (dark roux or espagnole).
Whipped egg yolk dessert or sauce flavored with wine. Italian 'zabaglione'. Light, creamy, luxurious.
Tomatillo-based sauce used for tacos, enchiladas, and dips. Spanish 'green sauce'. Fresh, bright, tangy flavor.
Thinly sliced raw fish served without rice. Japanese 'pierced flesh'. Showcases fish quality. Served with soy, wasabi, and garnish.
A liquid or semi-liquid preparation served with food to enhance flavor, add moisture, or provide visual appeal. Can be hot or cold, thin or thick, and derived from broths, emulsions, or reductions.
Cooking method where food is cooked quickly in a small amount of fat over high heat, typically in a shallow pan. French term meaning 'jumped'.
A fragrant oil made from fried green onions used to flavor noodles and proteins. Infused oil with aromatic quality. Key to many Asian preparations.
A flaky shell-shaped pastry filled with ricotta. Italian 'small, thin leaf'. Neapolitan specialty. Crispy, sweet.
Thin slices of meat swished in boiling broth tableside. Japanese onomatopoeia for boiling sound. Interactive, light, elegant.
A fermented rice wine used in marinades and stir-frying. Named after Shaoxing in Zhejiang province. Adds depth and complexity to dishes.
A fermented seasoning made from koji (cultured rice), salt, and other ingredients. Japanese. Tenderizes meat and enhances umami. Probiotic-rich.
Japanese soy sauce - salt-fermented sauce made from soybeans and wheat. Complex umami flavor. Essential Japanese seasoning.
A spice from Sichuan province that creates a numbing, tingling sensation known as 'ma'. Not actually a pepper but a berry. Essential in Sichuan cuisine.
Cooking method using gentle heat with small, occasional bubbles breaking the surface (around 180-205°F / 82-96°C). Slower than boiling, maintains texture better for delicate foods.
Cooking just below boiling point (180-205°F/82-96°C). Maintains gentle cooking without vigorous bubbles. Essential for stocks, sauces, soups.
In paella, the intentionally caramelized, crispy bottom layer of rice. Creates desirable flavor and texture. Achieved through careful high heat control.
Italian aromatic base: diced onion, celery, carrot slowly cooked in olive oil. Foundation of soups, sauces, and stews. Similar to French mirepoix.
Spanish aromatic base similar to soffritto: onion, garlic, tomato, peppers cooked in olive oil. Foundation of Spanish and Latin American dishes.
Thick masa cakes topped with beans, meat, and vegetables. Mexican 'thick little thing'. Crispy outside, soft inside.
Low-temperature cooking in vacuum-sealed bags for precision control. French 'under vacuum'. Exact temperature, consistent texture.
Extracting intense flavors using low-temperature water baths. Modernist technique. Precise flavor extraction.
Creating gel-coated liquid spheres using sodium alginate and calcium. Modern gastronomy technique. Creates caviar-like spheres.
Cooking in stages to optimize texture and moisture retention. Modern technique. Combines different cooking methods.
Recrystallization of gelatinized starch causing staling. Food chemistry. Explains bread staling.
Cooking food using steam without direct contact with boiling water. Preserves nutrients and delicate flavors. Universal technique across Asian cuisines.
Asian cooking technique combining high heat, constant movement, and quick cooking. Creates tender-crisp vegetables and fully cooked proteins.
Hot pot with beef, tofu, and vegetables simmered in sweet soy broth. Japanese 'cook what you like'. Interactive, communal dining.
A tangy spice made from dried and ground sumac berries. Bright, lemony flavor without acidity. Used in Middle Eastern and Mediterranean cuisines.
Water expulsion from gels such as custards or yogurt. Greek 'contracting'. Indicates instability or aging.
Long flat ribbons of pasta from Emilia-Romagna. Italian 'to cut'. Excellent for meat and cream sauces.
Ball-shaped grilled batter filled with octopus and toppings. Japanese 'octopus grill'. Crispy outside, creamy inside. Street food.
Steamed masa dough filled with meats or sweets, wrapped in leaves. Nahuatl 'tamalli'. Portable, festive, diverse.
Clay oven used in Indian and Pakistani cuisine. High heat (around 900°F/480°C) creates breads and meats with charred, smoky flavor. Ancient cooking method.
Olive, caper, and anchovy paste spread. French from 'tapenas' (capers). Salty, briny, intense.
Crepe-like dish made from cassava starch. Tupi origin. Can be sweet or savory, versatile.
A concentrated sauce used to season ramen and grilled dishes. Japanese seasoning base. Complex umami flavors.
Bringing ingredients to similar temperatures to prevent curdling or seizing. Technique for creams and mousses. Control is key.
Precise heating and cooling of chocolate to stabilize cocoa butter crystals. Creates glossy finish and snap. Critical for professional chocolate work.
Indian technique of briefly heating spices in hot oil or ghee to release aromatic compounds and flavor them. Also called 'tadka' or 'baghar'.
Light, airy batter-fried vegetables or seafood. From Portuguese 'tempero' (seasoning). Crispy outside, tender inside. Eaten with dipping sauce.
Ingredients like xanthan gum or lecithin used to alter mouthfeel. Food science. Creates specific textures.
Sudden temperature change affecting texture or integrity of food. Physics term. Can crack or damage.
Shredded chicken cooked in tomato-chipotle sauce. Mexican origin. Complex, smoky, slightly spicy.
Layered dessert with mascarpone, coffee, and ladyfingers. Italian 'pick me up'. Rich, creamy, chocolate-dusted.
Breaded and fried pork chop served with cabbage and sauce. Japanese 'pork cutlet'. Crispy, tender. Usually with tonkatsu sauce.
A cylindrical preparation of foie gras wrapped tightly and poached. French 'dish towel'. Elegant, refined preparation.
Measuring the resistance of dough or batters. Food physics. Indicates dough structure.
A crispy fried tortilla topped with various ingredients. Spanish 'toasted'. Open-faced, crunchy base.
Barrel-shaped vegetable cut used for classical presentation. French 'turned'. Seven-sided, decorative.
Pickled vegetables served as side dishes. Japanese 'pickled things'. Variety of vegetables and pickling methods.
Cloudiness in liquids due to particulates. Latin 'turba', disturbance. Indicates clarity level.
Chewy thick wheat noodles served hot or cold with flavorful broths. Traditional Japanese noodle. Satisfying, comforting.
The savory fifth taste found in kombu, mushrooms, miso, and fermented foods. Japanese 'delicious taste'. Created by glutamates and nucleotides.
Ingredients exceptionally rich in glutamates like miso or anchovy. Modern culinary slang. Creates intense savory flavor.
Creamy dish made from bread, shrimp, coconut milk, and dendê oil. African Brazilian origin. Rich, aromatic, complex.
A mother sauce made by thickening stock (chicken, veal, or fish) with roux. One of the five French mother sauces. Velvety, smooth texture.
Round-bottomed or flat-bottomed Asian cooking pan used for stir-frying, steaming, boiling. High heat, quick cooking. Essential for Asian cuisine.
Cantonese culinary technique - literally 'breath of the wok'. High-heat cooking that sears food and creates smoky, charred flavor. Signature technique in professional wok cooking.
The smoky, charred aroma imparted by cooking food at high heat in a seasoned wok. Cantonese 'breath of the wok'. Signature of proper wok cooking.
Dumplings filled with meat or shrimp, served in soup or fried. Cantonese 'swallowing clouds'. Delicate wrapper with savory filling.
Chicken skewers grilled over charcoal. Japanese 'grilled bird'. Succulent, smoky flavor. Often glazed with sauce.
Fried dough sticks commonly eaten with congee. Chinese 'oil strip'. Crispy, light, elongated pastry. Traditional breakfast item.
A Middle Eastern blend of herbs (thyme, oregano, marjoram), sesame seeds, and sumac. Tangy, herbaceous, slightly nutty. Used as seasoning and condiment.
Sticky rice dumplings wrapped in bamboo leaves. Ancient Chinese festival food for Dragon Boat Festival. Can be sweet or savory. Glutinous rice filling.
